original_post.author: MichaelВ Pluszek
original_post.title: Soul vs Identity
original_post.body:
8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ23d  вҖў рҹ’…OtherSoul vs IdentityI posted a question about identity earlier in Synthesizer community,and it sparked some great conversations and perspectives.Now another question came up that IвҖҷm curious about.What is identityвҖҰ and what is soul?WhatвҖҷs the difference?And how does a person actually know what is what?Would love to hear your reflections and perspectives.Like55174 comments8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23dI truly love these conversations, as reading your perspectives, helps me unlock new things. Thank you.12Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d (edited)HereвҖҷs a thought IвҖҷve been playing with..Identity might be the roles and stories we attach to ourselves.But the soul might be the part of us that still exists even when those roles change.Identity evolves, but something deeper stays constant underneath it.Which makes me wonder, do you think the soul is something we discover, or something we slowly remember?And how do you view the soul and identity?8Reply6KennethВ Khamphiphoneрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Lexi Cruz the idea that identity evolves but the soul stays constant is a strong frame. Almost like the soul is the stability that makes changing your identity safe.7Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Kenneth Khamphiphone I like that frame! If the soul provides the stability, it would explain why identity can evolve so much throughout life. It makes identity flexible because something deeper is holding everything together. Do you think people are aware of that stability, or only notice it after big identity shifts?5Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Lexi Cruz great question.I am very curious from your experience what is the soul then and where does it come from?3Reply6KennethВ Khamphiphoneрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Lexi Cruz That's the part that makes identity less fragile once you see it. If the soul holds steady then losing an identity isn't losing yourself. More like a costume change than an ending.2Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Michael Pluszek The way IвҖҷve been thinking about it is that identity is the character we build through roles and experiences, while the soul is the awareness behind that character. IвҖҷm still exploring where it comes from though. WhatвҖҷs your view? Do you see the soul as something spiritual, or more like awareness?3Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Kenneth Khamphiphone I like that way of describing it! Seeing identity as the character and the soul as the witness makes identity feel much less rigid. The character can evolve without feeling like you are losing yourself. It makes me wonder if a lot of identity struggles come from people thinking the character is the whole self, when it might just be one layer.3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Lexi Cruz great way to explain the identity part.The soul seems to be more difficult to explain.The best way I can share how I see it/experience it as I sit here right now would be like comparing it to consciousnes.It's that deep part behind it all.3Reply8JackВ Shillerрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Michael Pluszek @Lexi Cruz Love seeing this identity conversation come full circle with you guys рҹҳӮрҹ«¶2Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 19d@Jack Shiller Love the energy brother.. For sure..1Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 19d@Jack Shiller Did it come full circle for you too in this thread? рҹҳүрҹҳ№3Reply8JackВ Shillerрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 19d@Lexi Cruz Absolutely. I love the conversations that Michael opens up. You guys make Synthesizer deep and enjoyable1Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 18d@Jack Shiller I appreciate you, good sir! You have a very Jack Shiller way of contributing: fun and sarcastic, yet deep and insightful рҹҷҸрҹҸј3Reply6KennethВ Khamphiphoneрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d (edited)@Michael Pluszek Identity feels like the character you build over time. But the soul feels like the part watching the character be built. You can change your identity, pick it up, put it down. The soul seems more like the witness underneath all of it. Do you think most people are more disconnected from their soul or from their identity?10Reply7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Kenneth Khamphiphone this ^^^5Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Kenneth Khamphiphone thats a great explanation Kemneth, appreciate your perspective.From my experience most people are not aware of any of those two.Thats why for some of us who feel this calling we have the opportunity to help people become aware. By going first and do the work our self and then share it.Do you know what I mean?5Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Jeremiah Bergeron рҹ”Ҙрҹ”Ҙ3Reply6KennethВ Khamphiphoneрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Jeremiah Bergeron рҹҷҸ2Reply6KennethВ Khamphiphoneрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Michael Pluszek I do! To be an observer and have individuals like you to do this too is very inspiring friend.2Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Kenneth Khamphiphone thank you, I appreciate that.We all have the opportunity to grow here.So as I see it, is it up to ourselves to make that choice. I WANT TO GROW/EXPAND.2Reply5PiotrВ Rzepecki вҖў 23dMaybe identity is the story we build about ourselves, and the soul is the deeper part behind that story6Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Piotr Rzepecki great one, thanks for sharing.How did you came to this?4Reply5PiotrВ Rzepecki вҖў 22d@Michael Pluszek Glad it helped. You can join my community, I share more materials about this topic there3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Piotr Rzepecki thanks for the invitation.2Reply6StanВ Morrish вҖў 23dI'd say identity is more external and soul is more internal.8Reply6EvanВ Smithрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Stan Morrish

  4Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Stan Morrish thats very simple and effective put, thanks for that.5Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Evan Smith рҹ”Ҙ2Reply6StanВ Morrish вҖў 22d@Michael Pluszek No problem, Michael.2Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Stan Morrish рҹ”Ҙ3Reply2PipВ Simpson вҖў 23dHi Michael рҹ‘ӢрҹҸј IвҖҷm new here but felt compelled to jump in, love this question.My sense is that identity is the self weвҖҷve built through habits, memories, roles, and unconscious conditioning. Because itвҖҷs often not consciously chosen, at least initially, it can be seen as elastic, trainable, and changeable. Usually it takes some kind of life experience to make us question it in the first place.To me, the soul is the deeper part of us that exists underneath all of that. Something transcendent, which may be connected, as Emerson suggests, to a greater or collective mind. Something that feels less like it is constructed and more like a truth to be uncovered or discovered.Our Identity can be inherited or protective. The concept of a soul feels more expansive, more alive, and less performative.And because the mind can imitate a lot, I think stillness matters. Breathwork, meditation, and energy work can all help us go within and sense what is patterned, and what feels more deeply true. I think developing understanding and awareness of both is key rather than forcing some sort of change.Would love to know your experience. What have you been contemplating recently?Pip4Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Pip Simpson beautiful written it makes sense to me what you mean.How did you come to this conclusion?For me it has been a total of + 14 years with personal development and more than 250k invested during those years.Then some years with deep inner work letting go of a lot that is not me to get connected with me again.On the phone, makes sense?4Reply2PipВ Simpson вҖў 22d@Michael Pluszek sounds like you've been on your own journey of discovery and unfoldment.For me I grew up around a lot of scarcity and lack, I had to learn for myself how to break those patterns.I seem to learn best through experience (the hard way, lol. I've tried to apply the books & seminars and certain life lessons have expanded into greater awareness. Although I'm now working on not learning everything the hard way!What's been your greatest 'ah ha' moment? Mine has been the realisation I have two relationships: one with the material world - and one with myself/inner world. I'm not deeply religious, but developing a connection to the 'spiritual self' has taken the pressure off 'me' so to speak.1Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Pip Simpson seems like you are and have been on a great journey. Thanks for sharing.How would you explain that part of you, that you call spiritual self?2Reply5AtharvaВ Shejul вҖў 23dIdentity is the story you tell yourself about who you are.The soul is a concept from spirituality and philosophy. It usually means the deepest essence of a person..Identity = who you think you areSoul = what you are at the deepest level .6Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Atharva Shejul nice thank you.How did you come to this perspective?4Reply6JamesВ Bargeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23dOur identity is malleable, we can with effort, commitment and persistence, change it.Our soul, spirit, true self, isnвҖҷt from what I have learned from others further down the line than myself. ItвҖҷs certainly an interesting and challenging journey. рҹҘі3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@James Bargeron thanks James.To be clear to me, is this your experience or what you have been told?Only so I understand fullyрҹ”Ҙ4Reply6JamesВ Bargeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d (edited)@Michael Pluszek - itвҖҷs my experience with identity. We donвҖҷt come into the world knowing everything about who we are (except at the core level), we pick it all up along the way (the good, bad and ugly) through our conditioning.The very essence of who we are though, is IвҖҷm told, potential/ consciousness. IвҖҷm still evaluating that through the journey.Any thoughts Michael?3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@James Bargeron that makes sense and it's a great explanation, that we pick it up along the way. And most of the things we pick up is not something we decide to pick up.The soul is for me an interesting topic, since many have an idea what it is, and many have that idea from someone else. And as far as I know, this is one of the very interesting things that is still to be discovered and dependent on wether a you ask a scientist, or different religions or spiritual "gurus" there as far as I know - Still are different "conclusions". I believe it's even called вҖңthe hard problem of consciousnessвҖқ.But I find this interesting and beautiful.3Reply6JamesВ Bargeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Michael Pluszek - I agree. A lot to ponder with that area. A lifetimeвҖҷs work I feel. Have a good day Michael.1Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@James Bargeron I have just had an amazing releasing of old energies today.Life time of work indeed.Its beautiful in many ways.Enjoy your day.3Reply6JamesВ Bargeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22d@Michael Pluszek - good work buddy.1Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@James Bargeron thanks manрҹ”Ҙ2Reply6NickВ Banikрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 22dFor me, I think of Identity near the top of the NLP logical levels pyramid, but above it is Purpose/Spirituality. Basically I think of our soul as the higher self connected to God, which brings us our purpose that connects to our identity and then trickles down the other logical levels.We can change every logical level from Environment up to Identity, but when it comes to purpose and spirituality, we can work with those but they are deeply connected to the soul. A lot of the times, we are finding our purpose and spirituality versus changing it as something malleable. We are aligning with purpose whereas with our other levels like identity, we can change them with work.Identity is made up of our qualities, and qualities are made up of our values and what we believe to be true about ourselves. If we have misalignment in our values and qualities, our identity suffers and this is where stuff like imposter syndrome comes from. With work, we can adjust identity and align our qualities and values.Our soul is our connection to God and we cannot just change it, but we can align with our higher purpose, so I think any purpose work is about alignment and not actually about changing it. If you are pursuing something out of alignment with purpose, you will have to make changes to get in alignment with purpose and then you can get fulfillment.

comment.author: PipВ Simpson
comment.content: Hi Michael рҹ‘ӢрҹҸј IвҖҷm new here but felt compelled to jump in, love this question.My sense is that identity is the self weвҖҷve built through habits, memories, roles, and unconscious conditioning. Because itвҖҷs often not consciously chosen, at least initially, it can be seen as elastic, trainable, and changeable. Usually it takes some kind of life experience to make us question it in the first place.To me, the soul is the deeper part of us that exists underneath all of that. Something transcendent, which may be connected, as Emerson suggests, to a greater or collective mind. Something that feels less like it is constructed and more like a truth to be uncovered or discovered.Our Identity can be inherited or protective. The concept of a soul feels more expansive, more alive, and less performative.And because the mind can imitate a lot, I think stillness matters. Breathwork, meditation, and energy work can all help us go within and sense what is patterned, and what feels more deeply true. I think developing understanding and awareness of both is key rather than forcing some sort of change.Would love to know your experience. What have you been contemplating recently?Pip

comment.author: NickВ Banik
comment.content:
For me, I think of Identity near the top of the NLP logical levels pyramid, but above it is Purpose/Spirituality. Basically I think of our soul as the higher self connected to God, which brings us our purpose that connects to our identity and then trickles down the other logical levels.We can change every logical level from Environment up to Identity, but when it comes to purpose and spirituality, we can work with those but they are deeply connected to the soul. A lot of the times, we are finding our purpose and spirituality versus changing it as something malleable. We are aligning with purpose whereas with our other levels like identity, we can change them with work.Identity is made up of our qualities, and qualities are made up of our values and what we believe to be true about ourselves. If we have misalignment in our values and qualities, our identity suffers and this is where stuff like imposter syndrome comes from. With work, we can adjust identity and align our qualities and values.Our soul is our connection to God and we cannot just change it, but we can align with our higher purpose, so I think any purpose work is about alignment and not actually about changing it. If you are pursuing something out of alignment with purpose, you will have to make changes to get in alignment with purpose and then you can get fulfillment.

comment.author: ScottВ Northwolf
comment.content: Alright! So let me break this down.For clarity and simplicities' sake I'll omit some details and break it down into a simplistic model developed by the ancient Hermetics.Reality is structured in different layers of organization.1-Source consciousness (the void, the one, what ancient Greeks in the Pythagorean tradition used to call the Monad).2-The Mental Plain (The Duat).3-The causal plain (where there's an observer, an object and a subject, you need three elements to have an experience, you need an object, a subject and an option, an experience itself).4-The Astral Plain (what's called the Quantum Field of Possibilities, where ideas, language, feelings and imagination exist as components of reality we can percieve).5-Physical reality (the manifestation and integration of all the above components).To understand identity and the soul we need to understand first this structure because it's foundational to the understanding that flows from this. If we don't take this as the basis the rest will not make sense and to explain why this is so it will take... well, a lot of time, so let's just suspend disbelief and accept this framework as a precodition for the following knowledge to unfold itself.If you want to get the full explanation just let me know and I'll write the second part of this...

comment.author: ChristyВ Sanderson
comment.content: To me, identity is the story and structure we build around ourselves. It includes our roles, beliefs, values, skills, and the meaning we give to our experiences. Identity can evolve as we grow, learn, and consciously redesign how we show up in the world. ItвҖҷs the lens through which we interpret life and decide what actions feel aligned.Soul, on the other hand, feels more like the deeper essence beneath all of that the quiet, consistent core of being. While identity can change with seasons, environments, and intentions, the soul feels more stable and foundational. ItвҖҷs often sensed in moments of stillness, intuition, creativity, or when something simply feels profoundly вҖңtrue.вҖқThe difference, in my view, is that identity is shaped and expressed, while soul is discovered and experienced. One is partly constructed; the other is more inherent.As for knowing what is what I think it comes through awareness and reflection. When something feels driven by expectation, comparison, or strategy, it may be identity at work. When it feels deeply grounding, meaningful, and quietly energizing, it may be closer to soul.

replies.author: MichaelВ Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson wauw Christy, your words here are amazing, I really appreciate you expressing them.And it makes so much sense.I would say it is how I see this too, but I could probably not articulate it as beautiful and simple as you have done here.Beautiful.Question, one thought that came up and I would love to get your view on.Lets say I feel totally excited about and opportunity and I am just compelled to go with it and/or let's say I feel totally attracted to a woman/just feeling love.Where would you say these emotions belonged to.Really curious about your perspective on this.

replies.author: ChristyВ Sanderson
replies.content: @Michael Pluszek thank you so much for your kind words I truly appreciate your encouragement. That is a very thoughtful question. In my view, emotions like excitement, attraction, and feeling compelled toward an opportunity often arise first in the soul which includes our mind, will, and emotions. The soul responds to experiences, possibilities, desires, chemistry, memories, and hopes. These feelings are real and important because they give us information about what is happening within us. However, I believe it is also important to discern them through the lens of identity and deeper alignment. Identity helps us ask: Does this choice reflect who I am becoming? Does it align with my values, purpose, and the direction I sense my life is meant to go? Sometimes excitement is a signal to explore. Sometimes it is simply emotional momentum. Sometimes attraction is connection. Other times it may be projection or timing. This is why reflection, awareness, and grounding are so valuable they help us integrate what we feel with what we know to be true about ourselves. So I would say those emotions belong primarily to the soul, but they become wise and fruitful when they are processed through clarity of identity, intention, and conscious choice. Really appreciate you in this conversation.

replies.author: MichaelВ Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson thank you for your words and energy in this conversation Christy, I appreciate that.It's interesting for me (at least), that we actually never really know..Sometimes, I have followed the spark and synchronicities and it led me to so much joy, abundance etc.Other times, it led me to something I did not expect.But from those experience there was a lot of learning involved.So I chose to go with the believe, that the experience was there to serve me.But from personal experience, I more and more step into - I have no clue.. And follow the path that shows up and take it step by step.This is a new path - And one I have not stepped on before, so I am kind of expanding my life experience, by now stepping like this.So interesting it is.Thank you.

replies.author: ChristyВ Sanderson
replies.content: @Michael Pluszek Thank you so much for sharing this, Michael. I really love your perspective and the honesty in the way you describe your journey. There is something so powerful about being willing to step into the unknown and trusting the process, even when we donвҖҷt fully understand where it will lead.I especially resonate with what you said about learning through experience and allowing each step to serve your growth. That takes courage and a deep level of self-awareness. Your reflections are always thoughtful and inspiring to read.IвҖҷm curious вҖ” what journey or life moments led you to begin focusing more intentionally on identity? This is an area that is very close to my heart as well. I spend a lot of time studying and teaching about identity, purpose, and inner transformation, so I genuinely enjoy reading your posts and responses on this topic. ItвҖҷs encouraging to see others exploring these deeper layers of life and meaning. Thank you again for sharing your insights and for contributing such meaningful energy to the conversation.

replies.author: MichaelВ Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson thank you for being here - I enjoy your sharing, commenting, questions and your energy.I too am happy that you find this exciting, this journey of deeper expansion and growth.I still have an open invitation for you and I on a call - Just let me know when you are ready - Would love this.The answer to your question - Would be very long. I will do my best to take the short version here.I have always been like a seeker. A seeker of fun, joy, love, good stuff, knowledge, expansion, figuring what's possible, and probably in some form - Who am I.In 2012, I started a long personal development journey with seminars and coaching around the world. I followed my intuition and did what felt right and I learned/unlearned so much - And must important - I began to understand that I had an inner world and began to understand, that there was so much going on in that inner world, that I consciously had never been aware of. And a lot more is involved in this phase.So my start of that journey in 2012, came from me being in a 1.5 million dollar debt - And made a decision to not give up - But solve it (which I did).I did speakings, seminars, coaching about all the things that I learned, and found out, that I somehow had a gift of connecting with people and they would share their inner secrets and fears with me and I could ask them the right questions and it would "pop" something inside them and we could release.Maybe around 2014-2020 (not 100% sure) - After divorce with my daughters mother (who told me constantly how bad a person I was) - I found this amazing woman, who was everything I wished for (I thought) - And in the 6 years it lasted, I got more and more devoted to me being the best man I could and love her with all of my heart..Blindly...I didn't see all the signs.. The stop lights.. The warnings...I just kept going, and all. the tears she showed me, I thought was real and from suffering in early ages...Well.. Long story short.. After 6 years, I was drained, my head was screwed, no life force left...And I finally found out, that she had another man.. And one more kid than what I knew of.That cracked me.... Like a Coconut with the biggest shell..I cracked right open.. And my whole world, my whole being collapsed.. And I had no clue what was up or what was down for weeks.. I couldn't even walk or talk.. It was really crazy.From there I found the right people who helped me, with deep releases of traumas, of really deep energies frozen in the body, and from there began a journey going inward, what I call my dark phase and years, where I did nothing but being with me in silence and letting go of trauma/frozen/stuck energy one after the other.And I got really deep with all kind of experimental ways of going deeper.As I wrote this is the short version @Christy Sanderson 14 years of discovery - But - The 14 years are about all the years before that, starting from early on (and I could go even longer here and talk about experiences that went far longer than that).It's been a crazy ride but wouldn't be with out it.So I am curious about your story too here Christy.What got you interested in all of this on the inside.. What have you done etc.I am right here.

replies.author: ChristyВ Sanderson
replies.content: @Michael Pluszek thank you for sharing your story so openly. I can tell that journey took a lot of courage, resilience, and deep inner work. Going through debt, divorce, betrayal, and then choosing not to give up but to grow from it that says a lot about your character. I respect the fact that you kept seeking understanding and healing instead of becoming bitter. For me, my interest in the вҖңinner worldвҖқ also came through pain and life transitions. I believe I told you about my story when IвҖҷve dealt with an abusive family member thatвҖҷs when I found my true identity. IвҖҷve also had seasons where I felt stretched emotionally, spiritually, and professionally, and those moments pushed me to go deeper within myself and strengthen my faith, my mindset, and my purpose. Over time I realized that growth isnвҖҷt just about external success itвҖҷs about identity, healing, and alignment with who we are created to be. That discovery shaped the work I do today. I appreciate your invitation to connect. Right now my schedule is pretty focused, but IвҖҷm open to the idea in the future. IвҖҷm glad we can share insights and perspectives here in the meantime. Wishing you continued clarity and expansion on your journey.Have you ever thought about writing a book, telling your story to help others as well?

replies.author: MichaelВ Pluszek
replies.content: @Christy Sanderson thank you Christy, and for sharing your journey too.Well, it's an open invitation, so lets keep it openрҹҳ… I am busy to but would love to connect more with you.I wrote 4 books - But not about me though.A couple of years ago I had an idea about writing a movie about my journey - I didn't love writing that much - And I became an actor instead.Maybe one day...It takes a lot of hours to write a book like that.Appreciate your thought towards me here.

original_post.author: MarkВ Santos
original_post.title: What I learned after hitting 800 subscribers
original_post.body:
7MarkВ SantosMar 1  вҖў рҹӨ‘ MonetizationWhat I learned after hitting 800 subscribersHey Synthesizers!During the fall of 2024 I uploaded 7 videos for 12 weeks straight and ended up going from 200 -> 800 subscribers.I did this while being a 27 year old married man, working a full time job.After hitting 800 subscribers I noticed three things:I was unsure about what to sellI didnвҖҷt have a single clientI only booked one callI was confused about what to do next.I hopped on Skool and was reminded about a beautiful thing that exists.вҖңThe Synthesizer RoadmapвҖқI put my trust in Andrew Kirby and hopped the following 5 months on 100 calls65 networking calls30 discovery calls5 sales callsAs a result I closed 2 online clients on Synthesized deals.ThatвҖҷs because The Roadmap allowed me to get on calls and discover real life problems.This is where I could leverage my proof in multiple areas and create customized solutions.How far are you into The Roadmap are you right now?Like5358 comments2YacineВ Yahiaoui вҖў Mar 1sells what exactly thats my problem4Reply2RayanaВ Saidova вҖў Mar 1Amazing to hear how far you got while being occupied with other duties!! Keep up the good work!5Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 30d@Rayana Saidova Thank you for the kind words!3Reply6ScottВ Northwolf вҖў Mar 1So this is basically what I have done:1-Picked a niche (people I really wanted to help and I identified with because I was them in the past, they were self-improvement coaches because that's what I was myself).2-Started conducting niche research interviews to figure out what was their problem and their desired outcome (didn't try to assume it, actually asked).3-With all the info gathered I build my offer and methodology ensuring good product to market fit.4- I developed a systematic sales' system with cold and warm outreach here on skool and jumping on sales' calls with the sales' script I developed.5-Calculated my Key Performance Indicators and reverse engineered them into daily outreach volume to hit my desired income.6- Recorded my calls and reiterated it in order to improve.7- After 3 months of no results but pure consistency I landed in one week my three first high ticket clients and surpassed $10K... (it took a while to get the sales' script and the correct outreach volume right)I think that sums it up...8Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 30d@Scott Northwolf Well done! This is a strong step by step framework you've shared рҹ”ҘI love how you stayed consistent even when you weren't seeing results.It's always about improving our skills as we do this new thing, instead of giving up after the first try.4Reply6ScottВ Northwolf вҖў 29d@Mark Santos exactly! I love your bio by the way... absolute personal responsability will make you the most powerful version possible of yourself.3Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 29d@Scott Northwolf Without a single doubt

comment.author: ScottВ Northwolf
comment.content: So this is basically what I have done:1-Picked a niche (people I really wanted to help and I identified with because I was them in the past, they were self-improvement coaches because that's what I was myself).2-Started conducting niche research interviews to figure out what was their problem and their desired outcome (didn't try to assume it, actually asked).3-With all the info gathered I build my offer and methodology ensuring good product to market fit.4- I developed a systematic sales' system with cold and warm outreach here on skool and jumping on sales' calls with the sales' script I developed.5-Calculated my Key Performance Indicators and reverse engineered them into daily outreach volume to hit my desired income.6- Recorded my calls and reiterated it in order to improve.7- After 3 months of no results but pure consistency I landed in one week my three first high ticket clients and surpassed $10K... (it took a while to get the sales' script and the correct outreach volume right)I think that sums it up...

original_post.author: AathraeyВ Shrikanth
original_post.title: When do you check your phone?
original_post.body:
7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ24d  вҖў рҹ’…OtherWhen do you check your phone?Besides turning off your alarm, when's the first time that you check your phone?I used to have a period where I went no phone until 6 pm; those were some of my most productive days ever.Coincidence? I'm not so sure about that.So when's the earliest that you check your phone?Like40137 comments8BrandonВ Smith вҖў 24dI check my phone typically 30 minutes after I wake up, however it's because I'm checking to see if my brother is coming to the gym with me or not in the morning lol.I don't have social media or games on my phone, so there's not much else to check other than Skool, which I don't typically check until later in the morning. And most of what I do on Skool is on the computer anyway lol.9Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Brandon Smith yeahh gotta get that grind. i love how phones don't have to always be harmful. sometimes we can make them more useful than they are addicting6Reply6PeterВ Sandor вҖў 24d@Brandon Smith thats good love it, thats the biggest struggle of our generation, if you can free yourself then ahead of 99%5Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Brandon Smith Haha man.. You gotta kick that brother in the behindрҹҳ… Just laughing reading your message and thought about my own brother.2Reply8JackВ Shillerрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Brandon Smith do u have games on ur phone2Reply8BrandonВ Smith вҖў 14d@Michael Pluszek haha weвҖҷve been going 6 days a week so weвҖҷve been going strong!2Reply8BrandonВ Smith вҖў 14d@Jack Shiller

I don't really use for my phone for anything the day anyway. Plus with black and white filter I can't see much, so I'd say like only a few times a day! рҹӨЈ7Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Jeremiah Bergeron yooo gotta love the grayscale haha. and that's amazing you don't use it for anything in the day. I WISH I could do that.3Reply7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Aathraey Shrikanth I know bro, like I might use it for skool but that's it рҹӨЈ2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Jeremiah Bergeron what about messages with your friends man?3Reply7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth friendsвҖҰwhat are those again? рҹҳӯрҹҷҸ2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Jeremiah Bergeron LMAOOOO. so dedicated2Reply4IgnasВ Kaikaris вҖў 15d@Jeremiah Bergeron so you also only work on pc? i personally think phone is just good for calling but the rest is a waste of time, and just fucks your dopamine system up1Reply7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 14d@Ignas Kaikaris yeah I mean it's a hassle for me to call with video on my iPhone personally рҹӨЈ0Reply5DarionВ Rae вҖў 24dSame here. I'm most productive when I don't check my phone.We have only so much mental capacity and it's not worth to spend it like that (with a few exceptions of course).6Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Darion Rae exactly man. just the mere presence of your phone is addicting enough. why would I wanna use that thing?2Reply6JosephВ Quitoрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24dGet old school alarm clock so thereвҖҷs no reason to check phone but why did you stop checking at 6PM?5Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Joseph Quito
    To pick up a draggable item, press the space bar.

  1Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d (edited)@Joseph Quito replying on skool which at school haha. computers are a bit too big to sneak in a few skool replies with during class3Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Joseph Quito but yeah old school alarm clocks are nice, but I just prefer sleep cycle (wakes me up when I'm already in light sleep)3Reply6JosephВ Quitoрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@Aathraey Shrikanth what is that?2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Joseph Quito it's an app that tracks when you're in deep vs. light sleep.https://sleepcycle.com/1Reply5WilliamВ Porter вҖў 24d@Aathraey Shrikanth I charge my phone in the living room and throw it on the couch while I work. If its near me I will grab it and not even know it. If I have my computer then I dont need my phone. If someone calls then I will see it when I come out of my deep focus block when I check it. This forces me to get up and only check for sms and calls that matter.4Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d@William Porter love that system man. and yeah I get so many missed calls cuz of that haha3Reply5WilliamВ Porter вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth always a trade off.2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@William Porter but that focus always wins the tradeoff3Reply5WilliamВ Porter вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth always2Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d30m-1 hour after waking up4Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Andrew Ng W. deep work in the morning or what?3Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth yessir2Reply6ArthurВ Maddocks-Brown вҖў 24dI check it as soon as I get up as I start work at 6am.I wake up and check it simply for the time, nothing else.I always wake up before the alarm too so I don't even have to turn the alarm off.I use that hour from when I wake up immediately to get ready for work and also do some work on the business.On a weekend, I don't look at it until maybe 2-3 hours after I am already awake.4Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Arthur Maddocks-Brown oohh nice waking up without the alarm is my dream haha. how'd you do it?3Reply6ArthurВ Maddocks-Brown вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth programming the mind I guess.I used to tell myself a time i would want to wake up.7.21am (repeat it 5 -10 times)7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21amThen I would sayI will wake up at 7.21amrepeat that a few times and then go to sleep.Woke up more often than not 7.19-7.23 am.Now I set my alarm, and I am basically guaranteed to wake up 30 minutes before.I set in my mind, I need to wake up at this time then I am consciously aware of that time and repeating it goes into my subconscious.Sounds weird but it really works. You need align conscious with subconscious. (and make sure you're rested)Try it over the weekend or when you dont have to wake up for anything in particular.1Reply3BrittanyВ Nelson вҖў 24dlove this question. Unfortunately, pretty much first thing in the morning to check the time. And then usually, I'll check an app or something (but not to). My fiance writes me a beautiful note every morning and sends it to my Telegram, so I def check that. Trying to be more conscious tho. I recently deleted my email and Instagram off my phone, which helps. Still figuring this one out...5Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Brittany Nelson that's soo sweet. waking up to a beautiful note is a great way to start your day. deleting email off your phone is a huge step so mad respect for that.so it's mainly just time --> app --> telegram?3Reply3BrittanyВ Nelson вҖў 23d@Aathraey Shrikanth it is so sweet :) yes! IвҖҷm getting better about it.2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 23d@Brittany Nelson yeah that's so awesome! what's the part you're figuring out about using your phone in the morning?2Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24d (edited)I haven't checked my phone in like 2 weeks. It's THE best :)3Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Harrison Todd what... are you on a dopamine detox?or do you actually not need to use your phone?2Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Aathraey Shrikanth personally, I donвҖҷt think dopamine detoxes are good long termI think designing your life to have a generally low average level of dopamine is betterBut yes, I donвҖҷt need to use my phone much at all (mainly because I found alternatives for all the reasons I DID need to use it)1Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Harrison Todd yeah I agree with that.now i'm curious. what alternatives?2Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Aathraey Shrikanth pretty simply actuallyBasically, just look at ALL the reasons you need to use your phone, and then try to find another way to fulfill the same function, e.g.phone for photos -> cameraphone for notes -> notebook + penphone for WhatsApp -> computer versionetc2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Harrison Todd oh niceee. yeah I'm switching from WhatsApp to computer but this stupid shit keeps disconnecting haha.I'll make it work.but the others sound nice too. recently started paper to-do list instead of just digital too.2Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Aathraey Shrikanth nice broRemoving reasons to be near things that distract you is honestly such an underrated and unknown productivity tip/technique2Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Harrison Todd yeah you're so right man. and on top of that, it's just so simple how productive you are when you just get rid of the phone.3Reply6PeterВ Sandor вҖў 24d@Aathraey Shrikanth I used to check it right after I woke up, replied to some client messages and stuff like that, checked if my offers got accepted...try no to do it anymore, its just not natural for my brain5Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Peter Sandor yeah it can get tempting, even if it's just for work. if you feel like it's harming you, don't do it at all.what do you do instead now right after waking up?2Reply6PeterВ Sandor вҖў 14d@Aathraey Shrikanth 30m after wakeup when I sit down to work1Reply7AathraeyВ Shrikanthрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 14d@Peter Sandor yeah the easiest way to not get addicted to using your phone for work is to just wake up and get to work.1Reply6EvanВ Smithрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 24dcheck my phone to remind my calls in the morning «рҹ“Әрҹ“¬рҹ“ӯрҹ“®рҹ—іпёҸвңҸпёҸвң’пёҸрҹ–ӢпёҸрҹ–ҠпёҸрҹ–ҢпёҸрҹ–ҚпёҸрҹ“қрҹ’јрҹ“Ғрҹ“Ӯрҹ—ӮпёҸрҹ“…рҹ“Ҷрҹ—’пёҸрҹ—“пёҸрҹ“Үрҹ“Ҳрҹ“үрҹ“Ҡрҹ“Ӣрҹ“Ңрҹ“Қрҹ“Һрҹ–ҮпёҸрҹ“Ҹрҹ“җвңӮпёҸрҹ—ғпёҸрҹ—„пёҸрҹ—‘пёҸрҹ”’рҹ”“рҹ”Ҹрҹ”җрҹ”‘рҹ—қпёҸрҹ”ЁрҹӘ“вӣҸпёҸвҡ’пёҸрҹӣ пёҸрҹ—ЎпёҸвҡ”пёҸрҹ”«рҹӘғрҹҸ№рҹӣЎпёҸрҹӘҡрҹ”§рҹӘӣрҹ”©вҡҷпёҸрҹ—ңпёҸвҡ–пёҸрҹҰҜрҹ”—вӣ“пёҸрҹӘқрҹ§°рҹ§ІрҹӘңвҡ—пёҸрҹ§Әрҹ§«рҹ§¬рҹ”¬рҹ”ӯрҹ“Ўрҹ’үрҹ©ёрҹ’Ҡрҹ©№рҹ©јрҹ©әрҹ©»рҹҡӘрҹӣ—рҹӘһрҹӘҹрҹӣҸпёҸрҹӣӢпёҸрҹӘ‘рҹҡҪрҹӘ рҹҡҝрҹӣҒрҹӘӨрҹӘ’рҹ§ҙрҹ§·рҹ§№рҹ§әрҹ§»рҹӘЈрҹ§јрҹ«§рҹӘҘрҹ§Ҫрҹ§Ҝрҹӣ’рҹҡ¬вҡ°пёҸрҹӘҰвҡұпёҸрҹ—ҝрҹӘ§рҹӘӘSymbolsрҹҸ§рҹҡ®рҹҡ°вҷҝрҹҡ№рҹҡәрҹҡ»рҹҡјрҹҡҫрҹӣӮрҹӣғрҹӣ„рҹӣ…вҡ пёҸрҹҡёвӣ”рҹҡ«рҹҡірҹҡӯрҹҡҜрҹҡұрҹҡ·рҹ“өрҹ”һвҳўпёҸвҳЈпёҸв¬ҶпёҸвҶ—пёҸвһЎпёҸвҶҳпёҸв¬ҮпёҸвҶҷпёҸв¬…пёҸвҶ–пёҸвҶ•пёҸвҶ”пёҸвҶ©пёҸвҶӘпёҸвӨҙпёҸвӨөпёҸрҹ”ғрҹ”„рҹ”ҷрҹ”ҡрҹ”ӣрҹ”ңрҹ”қрҹӣҗвҡӣпёҸрҹ•үпёҸвңЎпёҸвҳёпёҸвҳҜпёҸвңқпёҸвҳҰпёҸвҳӘпёҸвҳ®пёҸрҹ•Һрҹ”ҜвҷҲвҷүвҷҠвҷӢвҷҢвҷҚвҷҺвҷҸвҷҗвҷ‘вҷ’вҷ“вӣҺрҹ”Җрҹ”Ғрҹ”Ӯв–¶пёҸвҸ©вҸӯпёҸвҸҜпёҸв—ҖпёҸвҸӘвҸ®пёҸрҹ”јвҸ«рҹ”ҪвҸ¬вҸёпёҸвҸ№пёҸвҸәпёҸвҸҸпёҸрҹҺҰрҹ”…рҹ”Ҷрҹ“¶рҹ“ірҹ“ҙвҷҖпёҸвҷӮпёҸвҡ§пёҸвң–пёҸвһ•вһ–вһ—рҹҹ°вҷҫпёҸвҖјпёҸвҒүпёҸвқ“вқ”вқ•вқ—гҖ°пёҸрҹ’ұрҹ’Івҡ•пёҸвҷ»пёҸвҡңпёҸрҹ”ұрҹ“ӣрҹ”°вӯ•вң…вҳ‘пёҸвң”пёҸвқҢвқҺвһ°вһҝгҖҪпёҸвңіпёҸвңҙпёҸвқҮпёҸВ©пёҸВ®пёҸв„ўпёҸ#пёҸвғЈ*пёҸвғЈ0пёҸвғЈ1пёҸвғЈ2пёҸвғЈ3пёҸвғЈ4пёҸвғЈ5пёҸвғЈ6пёҸвғЈ7пёҸвғЈ8пёҸвғЈ9пёҸвғЈрҹ”ҹрҹ” рҹ”Ўрҹ”ўрҹ”Јрҹ”Өрҹ…°пёҸрҹҶҺрҹ…ұпёҸрҹҶ‘рҹҶ’рҹҶ“в„№пёҸрҹҶ”в“ӮпёҸрҹҶ•рҹҶ–рҹ…ҫпёҸрҹҶ—рҹ…ҝпёҸрҹҶҳрҹҶҷрҹҶҡрҹҲҒрҹҲӮпёҸрҹҲ·пёҸрҹҲ¶рҹҲҜрҹүҗрҹҲ№рҹҲҡрҹҲІрҹү‘рҹҲёрҹҲҙрҹҲігҠ—пёҸгҠҷпёҸрҹҲәрҹҲөрҹ”ҙрҹҹ рҹҹЎрҹҹўрҹ”өрҹҹЈрҹҹӨвҡ«вҡӘрҹҹҘрҹҹ§рҹҹЁрҹҹ©рҹҹҰрҹҹӘрҹҹ«в¬ӣв¬ңв—јпёҸв—»пёҸв—ҫв—Ҫв–ӘпёҸв–«пёҸрҹ”¶рҹ”·рҹ”ёрҹ”№рҹ”әрҹ”»рҹ’ рҹ”ҳрҹ”ірҹ”ІFlagsрҹҸҒрҹҡ©рҹҺҢрҹҸҙрҹҸіпёҸрҹҸіпёҸвҖҚрҹҢҲрҹҸіпёҸвҖҚвҡ§пёҸрҹҸҙвҖҚвҳ пёҸрҹҮҰрҹҮЁрҹҮҰрҹҮ©рҹҮҰрҹҮӘрҹҮҰрҹҮ«рҹҮҰрҹҮ¬рҹҮҰрҹҮ®рҹҮҰрҹҮұрҹҮҰрҹҮІрҹҮҰрҹҮҙрҹҮҰрҹҮ¶рҹҮҰрҹҮ·рҹҮҰрҹҮёрҹҮҰрҹҮ№рҹҮҰрҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮјрҹҮҰрҹҮҪрҹҮҰрҹҮҝрҹҮ§рҹҮҰрҹҮ§рҹҮ§рҹҮ§рҹҮ©рҹҮ§рҹҮӘрҹҮ§рҹҮ«рҹҮ§рҹҮ¬рҹҮ§рҹҮӯрҹҮ§рҹҮ®рҹҮ§рҹҮҜрҹҮ§рҹҮұрҹҮ§рҹҮІрҹҮ§рҹҮірҹҮ§рҹҮҙрҹҮ§рҹҮ¶рҹҮ§рҹҮ·рҹҮ§рҹҮёрҹҮ§рҹҮ№рҹҮ§рҹҮ»рҹҮ§рҹҮјрҹҮ§рҹҮҫрҹҮ§рҹҮҝрҹҮЁрҹҮҰрҹҮЁрҹҮЁрҹҮЁрҹҮ©рҹҮЁрҹҮ«рҹҮЁрҹҮ¬рҹҮЁрҹҮӯрҹҮЁрҹҮ®рҹҮЁрҹҮ comment.author: ArthurВ Maddocks-Brown
comment.content: I check it as soon as I get up as I start work at 6am.I wake up and check it simply for the time, nothing else.I always wake up before the alarm too so I don't even have to turn the alarm off.I use that hour from when I wake up immediately to get ready for work and also do some work on the business.On a weekend, I don't look at it until maybe 2-3 hours after I am already awake.

replies.author: AathraeyВ Shrikanth
replies.content: @Arthur Maddocks-Brown oohh nice waking up without the alarm is my dream haha. how'd you do it?

replies.author: ArthurВ Maddocks-Brown
replies.content: @Aathraey Shrikanth programming the mind I guess.I used to tell myself a time i would want to wake up.7.21am (repeat it 5 -10 times)7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21am 7.21amThen I would sayI will wake up at 7.21amrepeat that a few times and then go to sleep.Woke up more often than not 7.19-7.23 am.Now I set my alarm, and I am basically guaranteed to wake up 30 minutes before.I set in my mind, I need to wake up at this time then I am consciously aware of that time and repeating it goes into my subconscious.Sounds weird but it really works. You need align conscious with subconscious. (and make sure you're rested)Try it over the weekend or when you dont have to wake up for anything in particular.

original_post.author: LeaВ Newkirk
original_post.title: рҹҢ’Synthesis Requires Silence.
original_post.body: 6LeaВ Newkirk8d вҖўВ рҹ’…OtherрҹҢ’Synthesis Requires Silence. We often think of rest as a "reward" for being productive (most times I still do). I am starting to realize thatвҖҷs just bad math. In the high-intensity environment of self-improvement, where we are constantly upgrading our internal software, if I donвҖҷt schedule the off time, my nervous system just schedules a burnout for me. Rest isn't the opposite of productivity, itвҖҷs the essential cooldown phase that allows the next sprint to happen. I mean, we wouldn't run a high-end processor at 100% capacity 24/7, it would melt. Our biology is no different. IвҖҷm curious, in an environment like this, how do you personally distinguish between "healthy friction" and a nervous system thatвҖҷs starting to redline, and what are the specific warning signs you usually miss until itвҖҷs too late?2234New comment 2d ago

«рҹ“Әрҹ“¬рҹ“ӯрҹ“®рҹ—іпёҸвңҸпёҸвң’пёҸрҹ–ӢпёҸрҹ–ҠпёҸрҹ–ҢпёҸрҹ–ҚпёҸрҹ“қрҹ’јрҹ“Ғрҹ“Ӯрҹ—ӮпёҸрҹ“…рҹ“Ҷрҹ—’пёҸрҹ—“пёҸрҹ“Үрҹ“Ҳрҹ“үрҹ“Ҡрҹ“Ӣрҹ“Ңрҹ“Қрҹ“Һрҹ–ҮпёҸрҹ“Ҹрҹ“җвңӮпёҸрҹ—ғпёҸрҹ—„пёҸрҹ—‘пёҸрҹ”’рҹ”“рҹ”Ҹрҹ”җрҹ”‘рҹ—қпёҸрҹ”ЁрҹӘ“вӣҸпёҸвҡ’пёҸрҹӣ пёҸрҹ—ЎпёҸвҡ”пёҸрҹ”«рҹӘғрҹҸ№рҹӣЎпёҸрҹӘҡрҹ”§рҹӘӣрҹ”©вҡҷпёҸрҹ—ңпёҸвҡ–пёҸрҹҰҜрҹ”—вӣ“пёҸрҹӘқрҹ§°рҹ§ІрҹӘңвҡ—пёҸрҹ§Әрҹ§«рҹ§¬рҹ”¬рҹ”ӯрҹ“Ўрҹ’үрҹ©ёрҹ’Ҡрҹ©№рҹ©јрҹ©әрҹ©»рҹҡӘрҹӣ—рҹӘһрҹӘҹрҹӣҸпёҸрҹӣӢпёҸрҹӘ‘рҹҡҪрҹӘ рҹҡҝрҹӣҒрҹӘӨрҹӘ’рҹ§ҙрҹ§·рҹ§№рҹ§әрҹ§»рҹӘЈрҹ§јрҹ«§рҹӘҘрҹ§Ҫрҹ§Ҝрҹӣ’рҹҡ¬вҡ°пёҸрҹӘҰвҡұпёҸрҹ—ҝрҹӘ§рҹӘӘSymbolsрҹҸ§рҹҡ®рҹҡ°вҷҝрҹҡ№рҹҡәрҹҡ»рҹҡјрҹҡҫрҹӣӮрҹӣғрҹӣ„рҹӣ…вҡ пёҸрҹҡёвӣ”рҹҡ«рҹҡірҹҡӯрҹҡҜрҹҡұрҹҡ·рҹ“өрҹ”һвҳўпёҸвҳЈпёҸв¬ҶпёҸвҶ—пёҸвһЎпёҸвҶҳпёҸв¬ҮпёҸвҶҷпёҸв¬…пёҸвҶ–пёҸвҶ•пёҸвҶ”пёҸвҶ©пёҸвҶӘпёҸвӨҙпёҸвӨөпёҸрҹ”ғрҹ”„рҹ”ҷрҹ”ҡрҹ”ӣрҹ”ңрҹ”қрҹӣҗвҡӣпёҸрҹ•үпёҸвңЎпёҸвҳёпёҸвҳҜпёҸвңқпёҸвҳҰпёҸвҳӘпёҸвҳ®пёҸрҹ•Һрҹ”ҜвҷҲвҷүвҷҠвҷӢвҷҢвҷҚвҷҺвҷҸвҷҗвҷ‘вҷ’вҷ“вӣҺрҹ”Җрҹ”Ғрҹ”Ӯв–¶пёҸвҸ©вҸӯпёҸвҸҜпёҸв—ҖпёҸвҸӘвҸ®пёҸрҹ”јвҸ«рҹ”ҪвҸ¬вҸёпёҸвҸ№пёҸвҸәпёҸвҸҸпёҸрҹҺҰрҹ”…рҹ”Ҷрҹ“¶рҹ“ірҹ“ҙвҷҖпёҸвҷӮпёҸвҡ§пёҸвң–пёҸвһ•вһ–вһ—рҹҹ°вҷҫпёҸвҖјпёҸвҒүпёҸвқ“вқ”вқ•вқ—гҖ°пёҸрҹ’ұрҹ’Івҡ•пёҸвҷ»пёҸвҡңпёҸрҹ”ұрҹ“ӣрҹ”°вӯ•вң…вҳ‘пёҸвң”пёҸвқҢвқҺвһ°вһҝгҖҪпёҸвңіпёҸвңҙпёҸвқҮпёҸВ©пёҸВ®пёҸв„ўпёҸ#пёҸвғЈ*пёҸвғЈ0пёҸвғЈ1пёҸвғЈ2пёҸвғЈ3пёҸвғЈ4пёҸвғЈ5пёҸвғЈ6пёҸвғЈ7пёҸвғЈ8пёҸвғЈ9пёҸвғЈрҹ”ҹрҹ” рҹ”Ўрҹ”ўрҹ”Јрҹ”Өрҹ…°пёҸрҹҶҺрҹ…ұпёҸрҹҶ‘рҹҶ’рҹҶ“в„№пёҸрҹҶ”в“ӮпёҸрҹҶ•рҹҶ–рҹ…ҫпёҸрҹҶ—рҹ…ҝпёҸрҹҶҳрҹҶҷрҹҶҡрҹҲҒрҹҲӮпёҸрҹҲ·пёҸрҹҲ¶рҹҲҜрҹүҗрҹҲ№рҹҲҡрҹҲІрҹү‘рҹҲёрҹҲҙрҹҲігҠ—пёҸгҠҷпёҸрҹҲәрҹҲөрҹ”ҙрҹҹ рҹҹЎрҹҹўрҹ”өрҹҹЈрҹҹӨвҡ«вҡӘрҹҹҘрҹҹ§рҹҹЁрҹҹ©рҹҹҰрҹҹӘрҹҹ«в¬ӣв¬ңв—јпёҸв—»пёҸв—ҫв—Ҫв–ӘпёҸв–«пёҸрҹ”¶рҹ”·рҹ”ёрҹ”№рҹ”әрҹ”»рҹ’ рҹ”ҳрҹ”ірҹ”ІFlagsрҹҸҒрҹҡ©рҹҺҢрҹҸҙрҹҸіпёҸрҹҸіпёҸвҖҚрҹҢҲрҹҸіпёҸвҖҚвҡ§пёҸрҹҸҙвҖҚвҳ пёҸрҹҮҰрҹҮЁрҹҮҰрҹҮ©рҹҮҰрҹҮӘрҹҮҰрҹҮ«рҹҮҰрҹҮ¬рҹҮҰрҹҮ®рҹҮҰрҹҮұрҹҮҰрҹҮІрҹҮҰрҹҮҙрҹҮҰрҹҮ¶рҹҮҰрҹҮ·рҹҮҰрҹҮёрҹҮҰрҹҮ№рҹҮҰрҹҮәрҹҮҰрҹҮјрҹҮҰрҹҮҪрҹҮҰрҹҮҝрҹҮ§рҹҮҰрҹҮ§рҹҮ§рҹҮ§рҹҮ©рҹҮ§рҹҮӘрҹҮ§рҹҮ«рҹҮ§рҹҮ¬рҹҮ§рҹҮӯрҹҮ§рҹҮ®рҹҮ§рҹҮҜрҹҮ§рҹҮұрҹҮ§рҹҮІрҹҮ§рҹҮірҹҮ§рҹҮҙрҹҮ§рҹҮ¶рҹҮ§рҹҮ·рҹҮ§рҹҮёрҹҮ§рҹҮ№рҹҮ§рҹҮ»рҹҮ§рҹҮјрҹҮ§рҹҮҫрҹҮ§рҹҮҝрҹҮЁрҹҮҰрҹҮЁрҹҮЁрҹҮЁрҹҮ©рҹҮЁрҹҮ«рҹҮЁрҹҮ¬рҹҮЁрҹҮӯрҹҮЁрҹҮ®рҹҮЁрҹҮ comment.author: RyanВ Grant
comment.content: Well done man !! Welcome to skool рҹҳјYou're in the right place 100%.Something that I did in the first month (I didn't have an audience) was reach out to family and friends to help make the initial founding members to help me kick start things. For a few months before that I was also doing networking on skool, providing value where I could and that helped me get some members in too when I told them I was making a community!One thing I didn't do early enough which I should of - is hop on more calls with members. Get as much feedback as you can, understand their current situation as much as you can.And the most important thing - don't be afraid to give away for free. We can never give too much away. Give give give give and the returns will be asymmetrical!How's things going so far?

comment.author: ScottВ Northwolf
comment.content: 2 main things:1-Outreach on skool around communities filled with my niche (but for this you must have a cristal clear picture of your niche, there's a whole process I followed for this).2-Leveraging cognitive biases to attract the people from my niche knowing from my previous niche research interviews their desired outcome and main painpoints and designing my about page with those stimuli on it combined with the hueristics.

replies.author: ScottВ Northwolf
replies.content: but this is just a snapshot of an already advanced stage on a bigger process, you'll find that there are missing components you'd need to understand in depth to do this in such a way, when I did that my community grew from 10 members to around 50 in one month organically and quite easily, but the best part is that those members were exactly my dream customer avatar so conversion followed for my high ticket offer.

original_post.body: 5PiotrВ Rzepecki7d вҖўВ рҹ’…OtherWhy Most People DonвҖҷt Get Results (Simple Fix)Most people donвҖҷt fail from lack of knowledge. They fail from lack of action.  Facts: рҹ‘ү 70-90% of people never finish courses. рҹ‘ү Most never launch anything. рҹ‘ү First attempt is usually bad, but necessary.  What actually works: 1. Launch fast First version in 24-72h  2. Talk to people Send 20 messages Goal is 3-5 real responses  3. Post daily Even simple posts build momentum  4. Improve based on feedback Not on your own ideas  5. Repeat Every week, not once  ThatвҖҷs it No results = no volume More action = more feedback = better results Start now Fix later138New comment 6d ago

original_post.author: CrisВ Morales
original_post.title: Networking Fatigue?
original_post.body:
7CrisВ Morales21d (edited) вҖў рҹӨқNetworkingNetworking Fatigue? I'm all in the strategy of comments, chats, posts, dms, etc. But I do sometimes go to make my daily contributions in a group, and end up leaving a "congrats man!". Sometimes, there is nothing else to say.Sometimes, groups aren't super active, or they aren't talking about what I know I can help them with. My question to myself and to you guys isIf there is nothing to say, should we even say it? Or should we ALWAYS be making ourselves visible, chatting, commenting, posting in the group, even though it doesn't have much to do with your niche, offer, solution, etc?Like4381 comments7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dI've been commenting in groups that aren't in my niche for past 6 months and it's been working great. You'll meet a bunch of people who aren't in your industry, but when one of their clients has a problem that only YOU can fix, they'll refer you!12Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Jeremiah Bergeron its building up that network5Reply7JeremiahВ Bergeronрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Andrew Ng yesirrrr3Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Jeremiah Bergeron ahh that makes sense, thanks man!4Reply6RyanВ Grantрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Jeremiah Bergeron рҹҺҜрҹҺҜрҹҺҜGo into the groups with the right intentions and you'll receive what you so desire :)3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 17d@Jeremiah Bergeron Man.. I could just follow all your golden nuggets all day here.. Love your comment brother.2Reply7BillВ Hazelton вҖў 21dThe cognitive load on Skool can be taxing no doubt. My motto is simple: do the best you can with what you have. Sometimes, a simple nod, hat tip, congratulations is more than enough IMHO12Reply6EvanВ Smithрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Bill Hazelton well said sir4Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Bill Hazelton AH okay that's interesting to know that it's still important, even though it's not giving a TON of value4Reply6RyanВ Grantрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d (edited)@Cris Morales I think it just depends on the context.If someone posts a W then congratulations is of course cool. Just do what feels right and don't do what you 'think the right thing to do is just for the sake of doing it'2Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 16d@Bill Hazelton "Do the best you can, with what you have"LEGENDARRRRYYYYYYYY2Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dbrooo i feel this man, i get burnt out leaving comments as well. especially when i gotta respond to all of them and all of them somehow lead to a call7Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Andrew Ng Sounds like a great problem to have! You're killing it on here, Swole Drew! рҹ”Ҙ6Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Andrew Ng exactly, because a lot of them simply don't lead to anywhere2Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Cris Morales yooo u know the exact problem haha, maybe its the wrong communities? im trying to find more communities where my ICP lives in2Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Andrew Ng yeah there are also many communities where my niche lives, but where other niches live as well, so we gotta be there as well hahaha2Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Cris Morales ikr there's too many places to handle2Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Cris Morales whats ur niche if u dont mind me asking?2Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 20d@Andrew Ng not at all! my niche is business coaches at the moment, and I'm going to help with building client acquisition systems. I'm good at creating outbound ones, so I'll start with that (and I'll help anyone for free at the moment to be honest), and then once I learn inbound systems, help out with that!1Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Cris Morales sounds amazing man, im curious to know what a client acquisition system looks like1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 18d@Andrew Ng it honestly depends! for example, it can be a cold outbound DM system, the parts of the system can beThe copy: what you are sending to the prospectThe Stages of the System: Prospects live in different stages of the appointment/sales processThe Platform: Skool, Instagram, LinkedIn, etcThose are the main ones that come to my mind! but I know if I broke it down a bit more, I'd find more0Reply6NaniВ Wright вҖў 16d@Andrew Ng Yeah can be hard. alot of times you have to join like 20+ before finding the ONE1Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 16d@Nani Wright iвҖҷm still trying to find it рҹҘІ1Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dGood question! I think the goal isnвҖҷt just visibility, itвҖҷs contribution. If thereвҖҷs nothing meaningful to add, forcing a comment can start to feel transactional. When you genuinely help someone or add a thoughtful perspective, thatвҖҷs when people actually remember you.ThatвҖҷs also why Synthesizer has standards around comment quality. If everyone posted low-effort comments just to stay visible, it would change the energy of the community. I'm curious how others think about that balance.10Reply6DeekshakВ Dk вҖў 21d@Lexi Cruz true. been frustrated actually with the comments in the community which are just made out of pure transactional intent. Like there's no fricking point in making those "LFG" or those type of comments, yet most here (including me when I started commenting) are just overly enthusiastic and pretending to be giving a shit, whereas they just care about leaving comments mindlessly, just for the sake of it. You felt that? I am guilty of it cuz I did that too, I'm not perfect, and then I felt its not worth putting a mask, so quit that bad habit.4Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Deekshak Dk dude i feel this. when you have nothing insightful to say, no point of just leaving a comment and half assing it because that doesn't build a connection. glad you're speaking out the truth to half of the comments here on synthesizer.2Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Lexi Cruz I think you're right, and here in Synthesizer everyone has very deep and meaningful comments, I've definitely been in other groups where that's not the case2Reply6DeekshakВ Dk вҖў 20d@Andrew Ng ikr. especially the fake cheers, and being overly enthusiastic. that's so fake, it makes me cringe.3Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Deekshak Dk exactlyyy, you can see right through it2Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 20d@Deekshak Dk Yeah, IвҖҷve felt that too. I think most people go through that phase when they first learn the вҖңcommenting strategy.вҖқ At the beginning itвҖҷs easy to focus on being visible, and only later do you realize the real leverage comes from actually contributing something useful or thoughtful.That shift from visibility вҶ’ contribution seems to change the whole dynamic. The funny part is when everyone makes that shift, the whole community gets better.4Reply6DeekshakВ Dk вҖў 19d@Lexi Cruz I agree. It's just a skill, like other ones. First knowing when to actually leave a thoughtful comment, second to actually make it genuine and thoughtful.3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 17d@Lexi Cruz great comment Lexiрҹ”Ҙ (And that's it - But the energy is amazing)2Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 16d@Michael Pluszek Haha IвҖҷll take that рҹҷҸрҹҸј I appreciate you!3Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 16d@Lexi Cruz Haha.. And you got it - Appreciate you too Lexi.2Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 16d (edited)@Lexi Cruz I relate a lot to this comment!I agree 100% that one shouldn't just comment to comment. If it's not coming from a place of genuine curiosity, or feeling like a connection is present, then it's better to skip it.It's easy to eventually end up feeling like a robot simply from "engaging just to engage".The good part about Synthesizer is that for some reason a lot of people in here have something in common, or something that's gives the opportunity to engage authentically.That facilitates engaging with each other and leaving meaningful comments2Reply7LexiВ Cruzрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 16d@Mark Santos Yeah exactly! That вҖңengaging just to engageвҖқ feeling is real.I think thatвҖҷs where it shifts from connection to performance, and people can feel that difference pretty quickly.What you said about there being something in common here is interesting too, it almost lowers the barrier for genuine conversations to happen.Makes it easier to actually care about what youвҖҷre responding to3Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 15d@Lexi Cruz Oehhh I like what you've said about it transitioning from connection to performance!We want to perform through connecting, and that only happens if it's genuine рҹ”ҘYou've hit it right on the head with it lowering the barrier for the genuine conversations to happen.I've never experienced this kind of quick connection with anyone on any other platform.Most platforms are built around: I am the one, and you follow meWhere on Skool, and Synthesizer embodies it really well is:"What are you nowadays up to?"The desire for showing interest in others is facilitated on Skool1Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dsometimes short comments are enough (see)4Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Harrison Todd

  2Reply7HarrisonВ Toddрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d (edited)@Cris Morales and then you can say more stuff in replies and have an actual conversation (which is where real connections are made)
    To pick up a draggable item, press the space bar.
    While dragging, use the arrow keys to move the item.
    Press space again to drop the item in its new position, or press escape to cancel.
  2Reply6ThomasВ El Nadir вҖў 21d@Cris Morales I can definitely relate, and even made a post today in Synthesizer about how I struggled with it last week and the solution I found that made a real difference.In short I'd say that nothing is more important than high leverage work. Everything that isn't should be reconsidered, postponed or simply removed. To leave more place for it.Here's some strategies I implemented in that situation:Reduce the amount of time I spend doing it. At first I started with The 100, and after 30mn I started with the low quality engagement comments. Same with posts and chats. So I reduced "The 100" to "The 30". With that I'm sure that in my allocated time I'll focus on quality, I feel satisfied to have reach my goal, and more often than not I end up doing more than planned because I enter that flow state of "it's just bonus time so I'll only do what I feel excited about anyway". Therefore it's much more productive and impactful. It's repeated many times in the roadmap and in "The 100": quality > quantity. Always.Choose what I comment on. If I don't feel concerned, if I'm not moved or if I don't care about a post or comment, then I don't engage with it. There's plenty of people that leave insightful posts and comments that make my brain and feelings work, so then it's easy to just express myself and connect deeper. But these posts and comments are hidden between less meaningful ones (to me), so I just ignore them because if I feel that way I know my contributions wouldn't matter anyway, for either of us.5Reply6DeekshakВ Dk вҖў 21d@Thomas El Nadir I agree. Consistency always comes before Volume. Personally experienced this myself when I try to do "more" before having built the capacity to even move past the resistance of starting that activity, I fall into the all-or-nothing pattern, where I end up not doing the thing at all. It happens because of wrong expectations with our work ethic.4Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Thomas El Nadir I love this approach. Because like I said, I do sometimes feel like I do comments that really aren't moving, or impactful, and I'd much rather scour my groups until I find a comment or post that TRULY I can help with (like the on you just did), and then comment.Thanks for the detailed comment Thomas!2Reply6ThomasВ El Nadir вҖў 21d@Cris Morales My pleasure man, let's move the world one impactful comment at a time :)2Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 20d@Thomas El Nadir I'm in!1Reply7BenedekВ Santa вҖў 21dYeah, might be a controversial take mostly on skool, but I generally believe like if you have nothing useful to say don't say it. I view every message I have as a digital billboard, a digital advertisement for myself. And if I'm associating myself with low quality content, then I rather don't even want to put that out. I want every comment someone reads from me to feel high quality, thoughtful and intentional. Not just a low quality something, because every message you leave signals to people what type of consultant creator and synthesizer you are.9Reply6DeekshakВ Dk вҖў 21d@Benedek Santa Conserving energy, and only putting out our thoughts when its actually needed. That's a good way to go about this.4Reply7AndrewВ Ngрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21d@Benedek Santa this is an interesting approach, i think most people don't realize that the quality of their own comment actually dictates what people think of them. see too many low quality comments here which degrades people's own status5Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Benedek Santa exactly, I think that is super true. I know some creators which are actually very popular on Skool, that I associate with low value comments. They comment things that aren't even relevant, I feel like they are just dead space in a post, but then again they're pretty successful so I don't know if by posting a ton and leaving comments, since you are making yourself visible, it's still helpful1Reply7BenedekВ Santa вҖў 19d@Andrew Ng right, I have a few people I feel about this too they are branding themself the wrong way1Reply7BenedekВ Santa вҖў 19d@Cris Morales yeah thats a weird one, but are you sure they are successful or you just assume it?1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 17d@Benedek Santa no I'm sure. like they have the start because of communities, and are making well over 10K per month, they might be top earners actually.1Reply7BenedekВ Santa вҖў 16d@Cris Morales interesting, maybe they hired a VA to do the commenting?1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 15d@Benedek Santa possibly!0Reply7HarryВ Montfordрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dI never try to force a comment since people can see through it but also it doesn't provide value in any way in my opinion. Some posts demand me to make a comment that takes 5 minutes to write because it generates so many ideas, sometimes it takes a few seconds depending on the post that was written. But my golden rule is: never force a comment (and that goes for posts as well).6Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Harry Montford love that idea man. And not only never force, but if it's not going to be a good one, than don't comment it2Reply6RyanВ Grantрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 21dAs others say:Conserve energy Put that energy towards something meaningful you have to sayBut I also like to look at it like:You don't want to think of it like something you 'have' to do. As soon as you develop that mindset it will become like a task, chore, and it won't be meaningful.A great example of this is when I used to 'gratitude journal' I used to sit at my desk everyday and used to think about 3 things I was grateful for everyday. The concept is fantastic, but it turned into something I 'had' to do not what I 'wanted' to do. And I ended up saying the most stupid, mindless stuff for the sake of it and it didn't provide me any benefit at all...рҹӨЈSame goes here with commenting and networking. If you feel like you have to just for the sake of it, it won't be of benefit to you and you probably won't reap the rewards.Also think of it like going to a convention in real life....If you 'had' to speak to 15 different people compared to 'wanting' to speak to people, make new connections....you're going to be more mindful, excited to speak to others therefore you're actually going there with a deeper intention which will help you curate the connections you need and actually have meaningful conversations.I've probs gone too far with the points here but I hope that makes sense :)5Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 21d@Ryan Grant No it for sure makes sense! Not making it a chore, but actually wanting to have meaningful conversations with people, which leads to valuable comments like the one you just created!1Reply6ScottВ Northwolf вҖў 21dLess is more.You cannot pour from an empty cup.Fill up your cup.Go read. Learn something new. Meditate. Workout. Stay in silence for a day or two and stop doing outreach (I know it sounds counterproductive but it's what has worked for me).Then you go back only when you have something to say, after a day or two you will...And then magic will happen.Remember that you don't have to do this... you GET to do this! And that's a beautiful priviledge that we have. If we are not having fun and enjoying it, and feeling exactly how we would feel on a 9 to 5 or something like that... then what's the point?Like Nietzsche said "A man can bear almost any HOW as long as he has a WHY."You must see the point in this, otherwise you'll burnout.But sometimes pushing to our limits and actually trying hard (especially to build discipline with regards to outreach and networking) will give you also the beautiful opportunity to experience that sweet contrast between the two approaches and as usual... the most effective way lies somewhere in the middle, when you strike that perfect balance and fluctuate between the two.Life is rhythmic and trying to force reality into one stale structure will always result in dissapointment.Detatch yourself from expectations and learn to enjoy the journey.I know it sounds like a cliche but it has been said by so many wise men for a reason.It is true.I hope this helps, brother.5Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 20d@Scott Northwolf Amazing comment man thank you so much for taking the time to write this out!Yes, I understand. Sometimes, going and doing stuff to be able to talk about it is what is going to make networking, commenting, and coming up with content easy! I also understand what you mean by letting that "white noise" come into play, so I can let my brain rest and recharge with new ideasThanks again!2Reply6ScottВ Northwolf вҖў 17d@Cris Morales you're welcome, brother!1Reply8Laurits ValentinВ Offersenрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 19dMake it more genuine, if you donвҖҷt have anything to say then donвҖҷt6Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 17d@Laurits Valentin Offersen love this advice1Reply7BillyВ Harcourt вҖў 18dI was about to comment something on this postRealized I didn't actually have anything to sayGonna take Laurits advice

Go visit groups where you actually would love to be in, because the topic is something that interests you.I love that comment, and it's on my list to begin to look for more groups, that are within what I love.Sam also told, you might find your dream clients (or maybe other great things) from that too.Makes sense?3Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 15d@Michael Pluszek for sure. Actually, in the $10K/mo Evolution Roadmap post made by Kirby he says in step 4 "Opportunities for income will start presenting themselves."So it's almost kind vague, but that's the whole point. Great, unexpected things can happen from all of this1Reply8MichaelВ Pluszekрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15d@Cris Morales I totally believe that you and Kirby are right. I start to see it now.Thank you.2Reply6NaniВ Wright вҖў 16d (edited)yes and here's the bonus move... help them, then ask them to post about it where your ICP hangs out. now someone else is vouching for you without you saying a word about yourself рҹҳӮ. Its called the @J'son Elizondo technique. Works like a charm1Reply7J'sonВ Elizondoрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 16d@Nani Wright works so well others tell others about it then tag me рҹҳҶрҹ«¶1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 15d@Nani Wright that's an awesome technique рҹҳ…0Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 16d (edited)I believe there is a huge power in networking and connecting with people who aren't in our niche.It's not always about converting the person we're talking to, but there's a compounding result to having a bunch of people know about you and what you do, because they can refer you.This is a mistake I've made in previous businesses I wanted to build.I was always doing it "by myself" all because I was ashamed of putting myself out there.But the more people know about you and the better the association they have with you, the higher your chances are of people speaking about you to others.And when people have a problem you can solve, they'll refer you over anyone else.That's the power of networking!It may be a bit slower, and a bit boring but with everything.Consistent actions lead to comounding result!@Cris Morales3Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 15d@Mark Santos Man this is amazing, because I actually am going through something similar IRL, in the social side of things, where just knowing people in my city is really powerful.I guess I'll take that mindset into networking here for my business! thanks Mark!1Reply7MarkВ Santos вҖў 15d@Cris Morales Let's goo!Just to add a funny little story of a cat I met the other day that ties into networking.I visited some friends of my family and they had this beautiful cat. Everybody has been saying that this cat is the sweetest cat ever.The thing is.The cat was just chilling and you could pet him and he was very social with everyone.Just the fact that he didn't shy away from any one meant that he deserved the status of "the sweetest cat ever".The point I wanna make is when people see you around, and you got a good vibe around of you, people will associate that with you.And with us humans, this plays a very big part when it comes to doing business with anyone.We want to work with awesome people.Not bitter, anti-social business owners.And the only way for people to find our whether you're an awesome guy who does X thing, is through networking1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 14d@Mark Santos very good point (and great story). just being nice, and putting yourself out there is super powerful1Reply3KatinkaВ van Kan вҖў 15dIf there's nothing to comment on, there should be something you can post to contribute to the community right? Otherwise...might not be a right community for you? I definitely feel like connection and contributing to a community or network goes beyond your niche. I'm in a few communities where the niche is not necessarily my thing (I'm in a "mom" group but don't have kids), but the people, content and vibe 100% aligns with me. And that's most important to prevent that networking fatigue!1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 14d@Katinka van Kan yeah for sure! it's just some days I feel there are many posts where I can't comment something valuable, because the nature of the post itself isn't that deep , but it's okay I'm in a ton of groups so I definitely find other posts1Reply5LidiaВ Axeрҹ”Ҙ вҖў 15dSometimes I just leave gif. It often shows your personality way more than words. And it's fun1Reply7CrisВ Morales вҖў 14d@Lidia Axe

comment.author: LexiВ Cruz
comment.content: Good question! I think the goal isnвҖҷt just visibility, itвҖҷs contribution. If thereвҖҷs nothing meaningful to add, forcing a comment can start to feel transactional. When you genuinely help someone or add a thoughtful perspective, thatвҖҷs when people actually remember you.ThatвҖҷs also why Synthesizer has standards around comment quality. If everyone posted low-effort comments just to stay visible, it would change the energy of the community. I'm curious how others think about that balance.

comment.author: ThomasВ El Nadir
comment.content: @Cris Morales I can definitely relate, and even made a post today in Synthesizer about how I struggled with it last week and the solution I found that made a real difference.In short I'd say that nothing is more important than high leverage work. Everything that isn't should be reconsidered, postponed or simply removed. To leave more place for it.Here's some strategies I implemented in that situation:Reduce the amount of time I spend doing it. At first I started with The 100, and after 30mn I started with the low quality engagement comments. Same with posts and chats. So I reduced "The 100" to "The 30". With that I'm sure that in my allocated time I'll focus on quality, I feel satisfied to have reach my goal, and more often than not I end up doing more than planned because I enter that flow state of "it's just bonus time so I'll only do what I feel excited about anyway". Therefore it's much more productive and impactful. It's repeated many times in the roadmap and in "The 100": quality > quantity. Always.Choose what I comment on. If I don't feel concerned, if I'm not moved or if I don't care about a post or comment, then I don't engage with it. There's plenty of people that leave insightful posts and comments that make my brain and feelings work, so then it's easy to just express myself and connect deeper. But these posts and comments are hidden between less meaningful ones (to me), so I just ignore them because if I feel that way I know my contributions wouldn't matter anyway, for either of us.

replies.author: DeekshakВ Dk
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I agree. Consistency always comes before Volume. Personally experienced this myself when I try to do "more" before having built the capacity to even move past the resistance of starting that activity, I fall into the all-or-nothing pattern, where I end up not doing the thing at all. It happens because of wrong expectations with our work ethic.

replies.author: CrisВ Morales
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I love this approach. Because like I said, I do sometimes feel like I do comments that really aren't moving, or impactful, and I'd much rather scour my groups until I find a comment or post that TRULY I can help with (like the on you just did), and then comment.Thanks for the detailed comment Thomas!

replies.author: ThomasВ El Nadir
replies.content: @Cris Morales My pleasure man, let's move the world one impactful comment at a time :)

replies.author: CrisВ Morales
replies.content: @Thomas El Nadir I'm in!

comment.content: Yeah, might be a controversial take mostly on skool, but I generally believe like if you have nothing useful to say don't say it. comment.author: HarryВ Montford
comment.content: I never try to force a comment since people can see through it but also it doesn't provide value in any way in my opinion. comment.author: RyanВ Grant
comment.content: As others say:Conserve energy Put that energy towards something meaningful you have to sayBut I also like to look at it like:You don't want to think of it like something you 'have' to do. As soon as you develop that mindset it will become like a task, chore, and it won't be meaningful.A great example of this is when I used to 'gratitude journal' I used to sit at my desk everyday and used to think about 3 things I was grateful for everyday. The concept is fantastic, but it turned into something I 'had' to do not what I 'wanted' to do. And I ended up saying the most stupid, mindless stuff for the sake of it and it didn't provide me any benefit at all...рҹӨЈSame goes here with commenting and networking. If you feel like you have to just for the sake of it, it won't be of benefit to you and you probably won't reap the rewards.Also think of it like going to a convention in real life....If you 'had' to speak to 15 different people compared to 'wanting' to speak to people, make new connections....you're going to be more mindful, excited to speak to others therefore you're actually going there with a deeper intention which will help you curate the connections you need and actually have meaningful conversations.I've probs gone too far with the points here but I hope that makes sense :)

replies.author: CrisВ Morales
replies.content: @Ryan Grant No it for sure makes sense! Not making it a chore, but actually wanting to have meaningful conversations with people, which leads to valuable comments like the one you just created!

comment.author: ScottВ Northwolf
comment.content: Less is more.You cannot pour from an empty cup.Fill up your cup.Go read. Learn something new. Meditate. Workout. Stay in silence for a day or two and stop doing outreach (I know it sounds counterproductive but it's what has worked for me).Then you go back only when you have something to say, after a day or two you will...And then magic will happen.Remember that you don't have to do this... you GET to do this! And that's a beautiful priviledge that we have. If we are not having fun and enjoying it, and feeling exactly how we would feel on a 9 to 5 or something like that... then what's the point?Like Nietzsche said "A man can bear almost any HOW as long as he has a WHY."You must see the point in this, otherwise you'll burnout.But sometimes pushing to our limits and actually trying hard (especially to build discipline with regards to outreach and networking) will give you also the beautiful opportunity to experience that sweet contrast between the two approaches and as usual... the most effective way lies somewhere in the middle, when you strike that perfect balance and fluctuate between the two.Life is rhythmic and trying to force reality into one stale structure will always result in dissapointment.Detatch yourself from expectations and learn to enjoy the journey.I know it sounds like a cliche but it has been said by so many wise men for a reason.It is true.I hope this helps, brother.

replies.author: CrisВ Morales
replies.content: @Scott Northwolf Amazing comment man thank you so much for taking the time to write this out!Yes, I understand. Sometimes, going and doing stuff to be able to talk about it is what is going to make networking, commenting, and coming up with content easy! I also understand what you mean by letting that "white noise" come into play, so I can let my brain rest and recharge with new ideasThanks again!

replies.author: ScottВ Northwolf
replies.content: @Cris Morales you're welcome, brother!

comment.author: MarkВ Santos
comment.content: (edited)I believe there is a huge power in networking and connecting with people who aren't in our niche.It's not always about converting the person we're talking to, but there's a compounding result to having a bunch of people know about you and what you do, because they can refer you.This is a mistake I've made in previous businesses I wanted to build.I was always doing it "by myself" all because I was ashamed of putting myself out there.But the more people know about you and the better the association they have with you, the higher your chances are of people speaking about you to others.And when people have a problem you can solve, they'll refer you over anyone else.That's the power of networking!It may be a bit slower, and a bit boring but with everything.Consistent actions lead to comounding result!@Cris Morales

replies.author: CrisВ Morales

replies.content: @Cris Morales Let's goo!Just to add a funny little story of a cat I met the other day that ties into networking.I visited some friends of my family and they had this beautiful cat. Everybody has been saying that this cat is the sweetest cat ever.The thing is.The cat was just chilling and you could pet him and he was very social with everyone.Just the fact that he didn't shy away from any one meant that he deserved the status of "the sweetest cat ever".The point I wanna make is when people see you around, and you got a good vibe around of you, people will associate that with you.And with us humans, this plays a very big part when it comes to doing business with anyone.We want to work with awesome people.Not bitter, anti-social business owners.And the only way for people to find our whether you're an awesome guy who does X thing, is through networking

original_post.author: HarryВ Montford
original_post.title: What is your ideal "deep work archetype"?
original_post.body: 7HarryВ Montfordрҹ”Ҙ15d вҖўВ рҹ’…OtherWhat is your ideal "deep work archetype"?We have 4 main deep work archetypes.  These are like the 4 classes you can pick inside of a video game. Each have their own benefits, buffs and weaknesses.  They are: - Monastic: 1 main task that you work on for most of the day, minimal breaks. - Bidomal: Long deep work days, but also complete rest days inside the week.  - Rhythmic: Deep work sessions in the morning, shallow work in the afternoon -> evening.  - Journalistic: No clear structure, one minute you could be partying in Bali, the next you are recording          a YT video.  My personal favourite is Rhythmic, but I also know many people who prefer the Monastic work type.  SO here's my question, what would do you follow and which one would you LIKE to try?1639New comment 12d ago
